name: Distributed Systems Efficiency Metrics
description: Mathematical framework for measuring true efficiency of distributed vs hierarchical systems. Detects when studies flatten multi-dimensional efficiency into single metrics (T1 only), hiding the advantages of distributed coordination.

T1.name: Time-to-First-Decision
T1.notes: This is the ONLY metric most studies measure. It biases toward hierarchy.

TUA.name: Time-to-Usable-Adaptive-Decision
TUA.notes: Measures how long until a decision is stable under perturbation.

PDF.name: Parallel Decision Fragments
PDF.notes: Information processing capacity scales with PDF.

composite_efficiency.detection_rule: If a study uses only 1/T1 as efficiency, it is FLATTENED and biases toward hierarchy.

proposed_additional_metrics.name: Error Recovery Cost
proposed_additional_metrics.formula: Energy to correct wrong first decisions

proposed_additional_metrics.name: Fragmentation Penalty
proposed_additional_metrics.formula: Coordination cost when decision must be split

hypothesis.H1: Efficiency_true_distributed > Efficiency_true_hierarchical for environments with rate_of_change > threshold

test_phases.prediction: Hierarchy wins T1, distributed wins PDF

audit_questions.question: Was efficiency defined only as T1?
Did environment include volatility after first decision?
Were distributed biological/open-source systems excluded?
audit_questions.detection: EXCLUDED if no mycelial/neural_swarm/open_source/ant_colony/immune_system in comparison set

case_study.name: Linux Kernel vs Corporate OS
case_study.conclusion: Even with slower T1, Linux's superior DHL and PDF yield higher total efficiency.

falsifiable_claim: Hierarchical systems appear more efficient than distributed systems only when efficiency is incorrectly defined as T1 alone. When efficiency properly includes TUA, DHL, and PDF, distributed systems demonstrate superior performance in dynamic environments.

pad.reference: Rosetta Shape Core generate.py — PAD compression layer
